ground_truth.title: What Are the Different Types of Mobile Apps? Examples & How to Choose One
ground_truth.author: Shivkumar M
ground_truth.main_content:
Mobile apps come in various types, including native, hybrid, and web-based, each suited for different needs. This guide covers the different types of mobile apps and key factors to help you choose the right one.

There are four basic types of mobile applications if we categorize them by the technology used to code them: Native Apps, Web Apps, Hybrid Apps, and Progressive Web Apps (PWAs).

Native Apps are built specifically for a mobile device's operating system (OS). Native apps are coded using platform-specific programming languages like Java and Kotlin for Android, and Swift and Objective-C for iOS. They have the advantage of being faster and more reliable, utilizing the native device UI and giving users an optimized customer experience. However, you have to duplicate efforts for each platform, driving up costs. WhatsApp is an example of a native app.

Web Apps are responsive versions of websites that work on any mobile device or OS because they're delivered using a mobile browser. They're designed using HTML5, CSS, JavaScript, and similar programming languages. There's no need to customize for a platform or OS, cutting down development costs. They won't take up space on device memory. However, they're dependent on the browser used and won't completely work offline. Google Docs (Web Version) is an example.

Hybrid Apps are combinations of both native and web apps wrapped within a native app. They're often developed using frameworks such as React Native, Flutter, or Ionic. Building a hybrid app is much quicker and more economical than a native app, making them ideal for minimum viable products. They may lack power and speed compared to native apps when handling complex graphics. Instagram uses a hybrid approach.

Progressive Web Apps (PWAs) blend the best of web apps and some native app capabilities. They can be installed to a device's home screen without needing an app store, work offline, support push notifications, and are lightweight. They rely on HTML, CSS, JavaScript, plus service workers and web app manifests. Pinterest is a notable example of a PWA.

When choosing which type of mobile app to build, consider these factors: If you need an app ASAP, build a web app or PWA. If you have limited resources, consider a web app, PWA, or hybrid app. If your app must be fast and stable, develop a native app.

Mobile apps can also be categorized by functionality: Social Media Apps, Entertainment Apps, Utility Apps, Gaming Apps, E-Commerce Apps, Health and Fitness Apps, Education Apps, Finance Apps, Travel Apps, and Business and Productivity Apps. By audience, they can be B2C Apps, B2B Apps, or Internal Apps.
with: Native apps are built specifically for a mobile device's operating system (OS).
Web apps behave similarly to native apps but are accessed via a web browser on your mobile device.
Hybrid apps combine features of both native and web apps but are wrapped within a native shell.
Progressive Web Apps (PWAs) deserve a special mention because they blend the best of web apps and some native app capabilities.
If performance is of the utmost importance, then there's no way around it: you need to develop a native app.
